results.title: Unrestricted claiming of fees due to missing balance updates in FeeSplitter
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit explicitly reports the same flaw: missing onBalanceChange updates on balance-changing actions allows new recipients with userFeeOffset=0 to claim all historical fees. Issue 13 (FeeSplitter Insolvency via Transfer-Based Fee Double-Claiming) identifies Curves._transfer not calling feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange for sender/receiver, and FeeSplitter.claimFees using the stale offset, enabling claim→transfer→claim or transfer-to-fresh-account drains. This matches the described attack path and root cause. The audit also covers the related deposit/withdraw offset issue in Issue 4. Fixing by calling onBalanceChange on all transfers (and other balance changes) would resolve the reported vulnerability.

results.title: Attack to make CurveSubject to be a HoneyPot
results.reasoning: The described issue is a honeypot via referralFeeDestination: _transferFees unconditionally calls referralFeeDestination (even when referralFee == 0) and reverts on failure, and the subject can set this destination to a malicious contract that selectively reverts on sells, blocking sells while allowing buys. The audit’s closest item is #22 (mandatory push-transfer to curvesTokenSubject), which concerns the subjectFee transfer to the subject address, potentially freezing all trading. It does not mention the referralFeeDestination path or the “call even with 0 value” nuance enabling a sell-only honeypot. Fixing #22 (e.g., pull payments for subjectFee) would not fix the referral-based honeypot. Other findings (#5/#17) discuss referral fee locking, not the honeypot. Thus, the specific vulnerability in the description was not detected.

results.title: Unauthorized Access to setCurves Function
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit explicitly reports the same issue in item #12: “Unprotected setCurves() Enables Complete FeeSplitter Drain via Malicious Contract.” Both the vulnerability description and the audit identify missing access control on FeeSplitter.setCurves, allowing anyone to point FeeSplitter to a malicious Curves contract to fabricate balances/supply and drain/inflate claimable fees. They reference the same function (FeeSplitter.setCurves), same root cause (no access control), same attack path (set to fake Curves then claim), and the same fix (restrict with onlyOwner/onlyManager). Fixing the issue as suggested would resolve both descriptions.

results.title: Malformed equate statement
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit finding #1 explicitly identifies Security.sol’s onlyOwner and onlyManager modifiers as no-ops (msg.sender == owner; and managers[msg.sender] == true;) without require/revert, citing the same lines and impact. This is the exact same access control flaw described (“Malformed equate statement”). Fixing the modifiers by adding require checks would resolve both descriptions, confirming they are the same vulnerability in the same code path.
